---
title: "Sourcing Delivery Model Assessment"
description: "Source note on delivery model assessments for public service sourcing decisions."
---

## Source

This note summarises Cabinet Office guidance on delivery model assessments [@cabinet_office_2021_delivery_model_assessment].

The main source is:

- [GOV.UK PDF: Delivery Model Assessments Guidance Note](https://assets.publishing.service.gov.uk/media/60a387d5e90e07357422eb45/Delivery_model_assessment_guidance_note_May_2021.pdf)

## One-Page Summary

The delivery model assessment guidance describes an evidence-based approach for deciding whether a service, or part of a service, should be delivered in-house, procured from the market, or delivered through a hybrid model.

For VfM rubric creation, this is one of the strongest sourcing documents because it directly links alternatives, strategic fit, operational criteria, cost evidence, whole-life cost, and recommendation-making. It supports a rubric standard that a procurement is not good VfM merely because the selected supplier is competitive; the selected delivery model must also be justified against feasible alternatives.

## Purpose and Scope

The guidance supports departments in creating and running an analytical process for delivery model choice. It states that delivery model assessment is not a target operating model, budget mechanism, full sourcing strategy, or supplier evaluation. It is a structured decision process for choosing the mechanism for service delivery.

## VfM-Relevant Concepts

Direct source claims:

- Delivery model assessment is an analytical, evidence-based approach to support a recommendation on in-house, market, or hybrid delivery.
- The process should define the service and delivery options, identify strategic and operational evaluation criteria, build or use a should-cost model, conduct evaluation, align cost and non-cost evidence, and document recommendations.
- Strategic and operational criteria can include strategy, policy, service characteristics, people and assets, market and suppliers, risk, controls, and transition considerations.
- Cost evaluation should consider whole-life costs rather than only immediate procurement cost.

Inferred VfM implications:

- Delivery model assessment supplies the alternatives logic for cost-effectiveness.
- It provides candidate subcriteria for strategic fit, operational deliverability, market viability, capability, transition risk, and resilience.
- It helps avoid retrofitting a procurement justification after the preferred route has already been chosen.

## Implications for Rubric Criteria

| VfM criterion | Delivery model assessment contribution |
|---|---|
| Economy | Tests whether cost evidence covers whole-life resource use and realistic delivery options. |
| Efficiency | Tests whether the delivery model avoids avoidable transaction cost, duplication, transition waste, or management burden. |
| Effectiveness | Tests whether the model is capable of delivering the required service quality and outcomes. |
| Cost-effectiveness | Tests whether the recommended model is justified against in-house, outsourced, hybrid, public sector, third sector, and other feasible options. |
| Risk and resilience | Tests whether risks differ by delivery model and whether the preferred model can manage them. |
| Capability | Tests whether the authority or market has the people, assets, data, and management capability needed. |

## Evidence Useful for Rubric Creation

Candidate evidence:

- service definition and technical specification;
- list of feasible delivery options and screening rationale;
- strategic and operational criteria with weightings;
- evidence base for each option;
- should-cost model and assumptions;
- sensitivity testing of criteria weights and costs;
- market evidence;
- in-house capability evidence;
- transition and implementation risk assessment;
- documented recommendation and approval trail.

## Relevance Rules Applied

Include delivery model content where it informs alternatives, criteria, standards, evidence, and trade-offs. It is directly relevant to rubric creation because it defines the decision frame before procurement or service redesign.

## Limits and Exclusions

Detailed operating model design is out of scope unless it changes the evidence used to judge VfM. Supplier-specific evaluation belongs in the [Bid Evaluation](sourcing-bid-evaluation.qmd) note rather than this note.

## Links

See also the [Sourcing Playbook](sourcing-playbook.qmd), [Should Cost Modelling](sourcing-should-cost-modelling.qmd), and [Testing and Piloting](sourcing-testing-and-piloting.qmd) notes.

Return to the [notes overview](index.qmd).

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
